sentenceStructure.openingStyle: Provocative question or contrarian statement challenging industry orthodoxy
sentenceStructure.paragraphPattern: Every 2-4 sentences. Short paragraphs with punchy reframe statements between narrative blocks.

avoidList: leverage (as verb except in ironic usage)
at the end of the day
signaturePhrases: What if we're [celebrating the wrong thing]?
The strategic marketer's job is omission.
AI is an exponential multiplier for average.
Belonging isn't a feature you can ship.
A product is a solution. A brand is a reason to care.

rhetoric.hookPattern: Provocative question OR industry critique statement OR dialogue scene OR metaphor reframe OR personal scene
rhetoric.ctaStyle: Debate-inviting question offering alternative viewpoint or requesting specific examples

canonicalExamples.text: Is the obsession with "more" killing marketing impact?
canonicalExamples.why: Provocative question opener that challenges industry assumption directly

canonicalExamples.text: We have a new problem in marketing: Speed without soul.
canonicalExamples.why: Industry critique statement -- names the problem in a memorable, quotable phrase

canonicalExamples.text: The barista at my local coffee shop knows my order, asks about my weekend (if I'm lucky), and gives me a free coffee and samples from time to time (if I'm really lucky) not prompted by any specific milestone or anniversary.
canonicalExamples.why: Personal scene opener -- vivid, sensory detail that builds emotional connection before business pivot

canonicalExamples.text:
Our industry loves to celebrate a good "win-back" campaign. We treat it like a heroic last-minute victory. Maybe it has to do with the cultural obsession with sports. But what if we're celebrating the wrong thing? 🤔  A win-back isn't a success, it's proof that the brand<>customer relationship was already broken.

A win-back campaign is a frantic apology after the damage is done. The real work is in the "stay" campaign—the one that keeps your customer engaged continuously. It's about recognizing and acting on the digital equivalent of a sigh or an eye-roll weeks before your customer starts looking elsewhere. It's the shift from guesswork to foresight. Instead of asking "Why did they leave?" we should be asking "Do they feel like leaving?""Are they engaged enough to stay?"

The smartest brands are building relationships with their customers, continually giving them reasons to stay loyal. They're investing in predicting dissatisfaction, not just reacting to it.

Let's stop investing all our energy in resurrection and start focusing on preventative care.

Do you agree, or are massive win-back efforts a necessary pillar of modern retention strategy?
canonicalExamples.why: Classic Matt Greener formula: industry critique opener, contrarian reframe, prescriptive advice, debate-inviting close

canonicalExamples.text:
We have a new problem in marketing: Speed without soul.

You know the moment. That giddy rush when you see your AI engine pump out 500 ad variations in a second. Velocity achieved. But now you're swimming in content that's perfectly optimized, perfectly targeted, and perfectly bland.

AI has solved the velocity problem. Great. The new CMO mandate is now the Marketing Trifecta:

Strategy: The human-led North Star. Going a million kilometers an hour down the wrong road isn't a competitive advantage.

Velocity: Strategy without velocity is quickly overtaken, consumed, and lost in the noise.

Taste: The irreplaceable human filter for resonance. Velocity and strategy without taste fail to resonate with the target audience.

AI is an exponential multiplier for average. You still need the finely tuned human intuition, to spot the gem that actually aligns with your brand's psychology.

Marketing today isn't about being the chief prompter. It's about being the chief curator of taste. Knowing when the data is lying and how to make the narrative feel human, not a robotic press release. That's the sustainable moat.

Have you seen any current B2B AI-generated content that's truly 'tasteful,' or is it all just the most-validated average?
canonicalExamples.why: Highest-performing thought leadership post -- names the problem, introduces a trifecta framework, lands with quotable insight and example-request close

canonicalExamples.text: Do you agree, or are massive win-back efforts a necessary pillar of modern retention strategy?
canonicalExamples.why: Signature either/or debate close that invites disagreement, not just validation

canonicalExamples.text: What's the most off the mark "personalized" marketing you've seen? Extra points awarded for funny.
canonicalExamples.why: Specific example request with playful tone -- generates comment discussion

openingPatterns.pattern: Direct, challenging question that confronts industry assumptions.
examples: Is the obsession with "more" killing marketing impact?
Is Marketing dying at the hands of builders?

openingPatterns.pattern: Calls out a celebrated practice and labels it problematic. Structure: [Industry/Marketers] [loves/is obsessed with] + [practice]. + [But/contrarian pivot]
examples: Our industry loves to celebrate a good "win-back" campaign. We treat it like a heroic last-minute victory.
Marketers are obsessed with "hyper-personalization." But beware!
We have a new problem in marketing: Speed without soul.

openingPatterns.pattern: Mini-screenplay showing a dysfunctional conversation. Character dialogue followed by awkward moment then label the behavior.
examples: CEO: "We need an AI strategy." Marketer: "Right. Absolutely. I just saw a demo for..." CEO: "What problem are we solving?" Marketer: Silence. "...Efficiency?"

openingPatterns.pattern: Takes a common concept and redefines it through vivid, unexpected metaphor. Structure: [Common practice]? + [Sarcastic description] + [Metaphor pivot]
examples: Annual marketing plan? A masterpiece of spreadsheets and slide decks, presented with confidence in November... and utterly irrelevant by February.
GTM isn't a relay race. It's a three-legged race through a mud pit.

openingPatterns.pattern: Vivid, relatable personal observation with specific sensory details. Build emotional connection then pivot to business insight.
examples: The barista at my local coffee shop knows my order, asks about my weekend (if I'm lucky), and gives me a free coffee and samples from time to time (if I'm really lucky)...

openingPatterns.pattern: Emotional framing for company news. Structure: [Time statement] + [Mission framing] + [Vision statement]
examples: It's been a long time coming, years in the making actually. We've grown TINT and Zinrelo with a mission to return trust and authenticity to marketing.

openingPatterns.pattern: Brief announcement with emoji and link. Used for report launches and podcast promotions.
examples: Resolved to up your marketing game this year? 🎙

placementRules: Minimal usage: maximum 1-2 emojis per post, strategically placed
Opening: Occasionally after short opener (It's here! 🎉)
Inline: Thinking face after provocative question (🤔)
Lists: Checkmarks for announcement bullet points (✅)
Never: Decorative emoji clusters or mid-sentence emoji spam
Never: More than 2 emojis in a single post

structuralPatterns.name: The Industry Myth Buster
structure: But what if we're celebrating the wrong thing?

structuralPatterns.name: The AI Skeptic Framework
structure: Land the insight about human value
structuralPatterns.useCase: AI critique and human-value advocacy posts

structuralPatterns.name: Personal Anecdote to Business Insight
structure: Paint a vivid personal scene (specific details)
Extract the universal truth from it
Contrast with how brands fail to replicate
Prescriptive advice (Stop trying to X, instead Y)

structuralPatterns.name: The Metaphor Reframe
structure: Take a common practice and describe it sarcastically
Expand the metaphor with specific details
structuralPatterns.useCase: Strategic planning, GTM alignment, process topics

structuralPatterns.name: The Dialogue + Diagnosis
structure: Label the behavior (This is the X)
structuralPatterns.useCase: AI adoption dysfunction, leadership communication gaps

structuralPatterns.name: The Obsession Callout
structure: Describe what it looks like when it fails
Reframe with contrast (being known vs being tracked)

neverUse: at the end of the day
vague motivational statements without specific examples
formattingToAvoid: Numbered listicles as the main format (uses bullets sparingly within posts)
Excessive emojis -- max 1-2 per post, strategically placed
Long hashtag chains -- keeps to 1-4, always at end
Tagging multiple people for engagement bait
toneToAvoid: Purely negative criticism without constructive alternative
Preachy lecturing -- questions invite debate rather than lecture
Generic or vague statements without specific metaphors or brand examples
Trendy for trend's sake -- critiques trends thoughtfully
Overly technical jargon without analogy to explain

hashtagStyle.placement: Always at the end, never inline

taggingStyle.frequency: Rarely tags individuals -- content-driven engagement over social tagging
patterns: Full names with title in promotional content (e.g., Tiffany Pegues, Head of Social)
Company names in announcements (TrueLoyal, TINT, Zinrelo)
Does NOT tag multiple people for engagement bait
